Bone marrow

Primary structural component of bone
Calcium Hydroxyapatite (HA)
Ca10(PO4)6 (OH)2
Consists of Ca2+ ions surrounded by PO42- and OH- ions
Review Article
The role of hydroxyl channel in defining selected physicochemical peculiarities exhibited by hydroxyapatite
Vuk Uskoković*a
Show Affiliations
RSC Adv., 2015,5, 36614-36633
DOI: 10.1039/C4RA17180BReceived 28 Dec 2014, Accepted 27 Mar 2015First published online 27 Mar 2015
Mysteries surrounding the most important mineral for vertebrate biology, hydroxyapatite, are many. Perhaps the Greek root of its name, απαταo, meaning ‘to deceive’ and given to its mineral form by the early gem collectors who confused it with more precious stones, is still applicable today, though in a different connotation, descriptive of a number of physicochemical peculiarities exhibited by it. Comparable to water as the epitome of peculiarities in the realm of liquids, hydroxyapatite can serve as a paradigm of peculiarities in the world of solids. Ten of the peculiar properties of hydroxyapatite are sketched in this study, ranging from (i) the crystal lattice flexibility to (ii) notorious surface layer instability, (iii) finite piezoelectricity, pyroelectricity and conductivity to protons, (iv) accelerated growth and improved osteoconductivity in electromagnetic fields, (v) a high nucleation rate at low supersaturation and low crystal growth rate at high supersaturation, (vi) higher bioactivity and resorbability of biological apatite compared to those of the synthetic ones, and beyond. An attempt has been made to explain this array of curious characteristics by referring to a particular element of the crystal structure of hydroxyapatite: the hydroxyl ion channel extending in the direction of the c-axis, through a crystallographic column created by the overlapping calcium ion triangles.

Types of cells that make up bone

Bone marrow
-Within the long bones are two types of bone marrow: red marrow and yellow marrow.
-At birth, all marrow is red marrow, fat eventually fills 50% or so of the marrow
-During starvation, the body uses the fat in yellow marrow for energy. With extreme blood loss, the yellow marrow can be re-converted into red marrow.
Head of femur showing red and yellow marrow
Stromal cells are connective tissue cells

Bone marrow is also the home of other stem cells besides those of the blood
MultiPotent Progenitor stem cell
Common Myeloid  Progenitor stem cell
MesenchymalStem Cells
Osteoblasts, and endothelial cells play a role in regulating HSC proliferation
Signaling Lymphocyte Activation Molecules
Important side note:  Term “B cells” comes from bursa of Fabricius

Diagram of the Cloaca of a Bird
BF      Bursa of Fabricius (Bursa Fabricii) ; CD. ..... Coprodaeum ; UD. ..... Urodaeum ; PD. ..... Proctodaeum R. ..... Rectum ; u. ..... Ureter ; v.d. ..... Vas de ferens
The term “B-cell” comes from the Bursa of Fabricius
An avian cloaca or vent; in this example, a red-tailed hawk (Buteo jamaicensis). Cloaca comes from Latin for “sewer”
The function  of the thymus is to generate and select a repertoire of T cells that will protect the body from infection
Thymus

Thymus decreases in size and productivity in adults
Weight:
30 grams to about 3 g
Cell productivity:
At 35, it is 20%
At 65 its down to 2%
Peripheral T cell expansion protects adults

Thymus anatomy
lobule
medulla
cortex

Book image of thymus
The capsule is  dense connective tissue
Trabecula means “little beam”
Thymocytes are baby T lymphocytes

formed from dead epithelial reticular cells cells arranged concentrically.
The function of Hassall's corpuscles is currently unclear, and the absence of this structure in the mouse thymus has restricted mechanistic dissection.
Are a potent source of the cytokine thymic stromal leukopoetin TSLP which directs maturation of dendritic cells in vitro. It is unknown if this is the physiological function of Hassall's corpuscles in vivo.
Hassall’s Corpuscles

Thymocytes
-Immature T cells
-Develop in the thymus
-over 95% die without reaching maturity

Lymphatic System
A network of vessels and nodes that conveys lymph.  It returns plasma-derived intersitial fluids to the bloodstream and plays an important role in the integration of the immune system.

Lymphatic System Function
1) Maintains fluid balance
-30 liters of plasma pass out of capillaries each day	-27 liters get resorbed at capillary	-3 liter left in tissue spaces get resorbed into lymph vessels
2) Fat absorption from gastrointestinal tract
-lacteals in small intestine
-lymph + fat = chyle (a milky white fluid)
3) Body defense system
-lymph nodes filter lymph
-contains lymphocytes
-spleen filters blood

Interstitial fluid
fluid found in the spaces between cells of an organ or tissue
Overall view of lymphatic system drainage

Filarial roundworm species are responsible for elephantiasis
Filarial roundworms are cylindrical, unsegmented organisms that measure 4cm in length for males and 9cm for females.
They mature and reproduce in various regions of the lymphatic system for 3-8 years.

Lymph nodes
the sites where immune responses are mounted to antigens in lymph.  They are encapsulated bean-shaped structures containing a network packed with lymphocytes, macrophages, and dendritic cells.
Names of lymph nodes
Primary follicle

mostly B cells, macrophages, and dendritic cells
mostly T cells and dendritic cells
many Plasma cells secreting antibodies

Spleen
Secondary lymphoid organ where old erythrocytes are destroyed and blood-borne antigens are trapped and presented to lymphocytes
Spleen

Spleen anatomy
Mostly T lymphocytes
Mostly B lymphocytes

Mucosa-Associated Lymphoid Tissue (MALT)
Peyer’s patches
tonsils
appendix

Peyer’s Patches
Lymphoid follicles situated along the wall of the small intestine that trap antigens from the gastrointestinal tract and provide sites where B and T cells can interact with antigen.
